title: Surgical Wound Drains: JP, Hemovac, and Penrose
subtitle: The nurse manages wound drains — restoring suction by compressing and capping a JP or Hemovac, recognizing the open Penrose drain, measuring each drain's output, and reporting bright red or foul drainage.

patient.name: Wound Drains
patient.admitDate: The nurse empties, measures, and maintains surgical wound drains.

nursesNote: The nurse manages surgical wound drains. Jackson-Pratt and Hemovac drains are closed systems that use gentle suction; to restore suction the nurse empties the device, then compresses it and replaces the plug while it stays collapsed, which creates the negative pressure that pulls fluid out. A Penrose drain is an open drain that uses no suction, draining onto the dressing and held in place by a safety pin, so the dressing is kept clean and changed often. Drains are emptied when about half to two-thirds full, and each drain's output is measured and recorded separately by amount and color; drainage normally progresses from sanguineous (red) to serosanguineous to serous over days. The nurse secures the drain to prevent tension or dislodgement, assesses the site for infection, and uses aseptic technique. A sudden increase in bright red drainage, foul or purulent output, or a sudden stop with swelling is reported to the surgeon.
providerOrders: Empty drains when about half to two-thirds full
Restore suction on a JP/Hemovac by compressing and then capping it
Measure and record each drain's output separately
Secure the drain to prevent tension or dislodgement
Report bright red, foul, or suddenly stopped drainage

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about wound drains?

opts.text: JP and Hemovac drains use gentle suction to remove fluid
The device is compressed and capped to restore suction
A Penrose drain uses no suction and drains onto the dressing
A closed drain provides suction when fully expanded
Drain output is measured and recorded
The drain is secured to prevent tension and dislodgement
Expected drainage progresses from sanguineous to serous over days
Foul-smelling or purulent drainage may signal infection
A sudden stop in drainage with swelling should be reported
A drain left expanded provides suction
Each drain's output is measured separately
The drain is emptied when about half to two-thirds full

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order emptying a JP/Hemovac and restoring suction

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene and don gloves
Open the plug and empty drainage into a container
Measure and note the amount and color
Compress the device and replace the plug while collapsed
Secure the drain and document the output

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Drainage suddenly turns bright red and increases
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the drain output, site, and the patient's vitals
Reinforce or change the dressing as needed
Notify the surgeon of the change
Continue to monitor output closely
Document the findings
Compressing the JP before capping to restore suction
Leaving a Hemovac expanded after emptying
Measuring each drain's output separately
Ignoring foul, purulent drainage
Securing the drain to prevent pulling
Letting tension pull on the drain tubing
Using aseptic technique to empty the drain
Ignoring a sudden stop in drainage with swelling
